speaker: interviewer
text: Alicia Appleman. You were telling me how suddenly you woke up after two weeks?

speaker: interviewee
text: I woke up after a little bit over two weeks. You see, what happened-- when he beat me-- crushed me like that-- they threw my body to the cemetery to be buried with all of the other people who were murdered. The people who went to bury us were from the ghetto. They dug the grave. And they went to bury us. And when they lifted me up to put me in the grave, I was still alive. Because the-- the water that I drank, or maybe I had already germs of typhoid in me-- made me sick. And I had a very high fever. So that kept me alive.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they buried me in such a way where when the Germans left, that they took me out of the grave, put me under the straw of their sleigh, and smuggled me into the ghetto. That was in--in the city of Chortkov. And who was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mr. Gold. He came with the burial people. And he took me to his home.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was he Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, he was a prominent lawyer in the city of Chortkov. Yes. And he took me to his home, and I was there for seven weeks. And they taped-- I remember waking up. And my body was taped, because my-- my ribs were cracked. And it's very difficult to crack children's ribs. And I was very, very ill with typhoid. And at the same time, I also had tuberculosis. But he had gotten help for me. Because I remember something bitter being put into my mouth as I was hallucinating. Because with typhoid, you have about 11 bad days of very, very high fever. So that's the crisis. If you live through the crisis, you can live. But I was ill on top of it. My-- my body was very badly beaten. I was hemorrhaging internally, and I had tuberculosis.

speaker: interviewee
text: But they did everything for me, Mr. and Mrs. Gold. You know their tragedy? They had an only child, this 16-year-old boy. He was killed a few days before I was taken into their home. He was caught on the street and just murdered-- just shot. So they gave all their energies and all their love to save my life.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they were very brave, because if there had been an action during that time, they couldn't put me in the hiding place. Because I would have cried out with the fever and betrayed the place. They would have stayed and died with me. But thank God there wasn't an action then.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then they made it possible for me to return home. They actually returned me home. Can you imagine? After seven weeks, I came home.

speaker: interviewee
text: But when I came home-- what the Germans did, and they did it in other towns, because somebody told me later on. They took people. They contaminated them with typhoid germs, send them back into the healthy ghetto to get everybody sick there. And then they were claiming that the Jews are spreading typhoid. They all deserved to be killed.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who told you this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Who called about?

speaker: interviewer
text: Who told you this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mr. Gold asked me, because he heard rumors that the people were-- this is what they were doing. He asked me if I was given water to drink. And I said, yes, because I was ill. And when I came home--

speaker: interviewer
text: Before-- going back to the cell, how many people were in your cell?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think maybe 30. I don't know exactly. But at that time, they took about 60 to 80 people. But they took them for apparently for this purpose-- to torture, and to train the dogs on-- on them, and to contaminate them. Because they sent about 20 people back from Chortkov, from that prison, to contaminate the rest of the people with typhoid germs.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you know about--

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- and they got sick.

speaker: interviewer
text: --these 20 people?

speaker: interviewee
text: I beg your--

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get it?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was told when I came back. I didn't know. When I came back and I said, what is this typhoid? They say that people came back sick, and they-- and my mother and my little brother were very ill.

speaker: interviewer
text: Going back to the cell, you said for dogs to be trained? The--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you explain that to me?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, what happened-- they were taking people out of the prison cell, and they were throwing them to the dogs. And the dogs were learning how to take them-- to tear them to pieces. Because when they came back into the prison cell, they-- they-- they were mutilated by the dogs. Some of them were still alive.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you saw this?

speaker: interviewee
text: I saw it, yes. I saw the people. And Mrs. Eckerling also told me what was happening. Yes. I saw it. I saw some of them. And some of them never returned. And the screaming-- at night, the screams were terrible. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So now after seven weeks, you're back home. Could you describe that day? And how did you get home?

speaker: interviewee
text: What happened is Mr. Gold arranged for somebody to take me on in his sleigh, because he had-- they had to smuggle me out. And it was very wisely done. They put me in-- in like a coffin, and they took all of the dead people on the top and brought them to take them to the cemetery. And I was smuggling out under the dead.

speaker: interviewee
text: So when they came to the cemetery, I was already well then. And I said to myself, God, am I going to be buried in that coffin? But it was just to protect me from all the other dead bodies. And then I came. And he took me.

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, there was a mystery about this man, because I didn't really know who he was. I have always suspected that he was a Jew masquerading as a Ukrainian. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. And what did Mr. and Mrs. Gold tell you when you left?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they just told me that I-- they-- they told me that I should try to live and I should try to survive. And they gave me their son's jacket. And later on, I found out that the pocket was filled with money. Because his-- their son had carried money with him in case he was caught that he should be able maybe to bribe himself out of the Ukrainian police. They-- they often took bribes of the German police. But when they caught him, he didn't-- he wasn't wearing his jacket. So they gave me the jacket, and they knew the money was in there. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] They were wonderful people. But they did not live through it.

speaker: interviewee
text: So when I came home, the first thing that struck me as strange are these signs-- 'typhoid'-- on the doors. And I came to our house, and I opened the door to our room. My heart was beating violently.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then I saw two bodies on the beds. I saw my little brother and my mother. And I came to my mother, and I saw her eyes glazed. I touched, and she had a fever. But then she turned around and looked at me. And then she recognized me. And she says, Alicia, you came home. And I says, yes, Mama. I came home. She knew that I was home. She had typhoid. Both my mother and my little brother had typhoid. And they were just about in the middle of the crisis. And, of course, then I did everything possible for them. I tried to get some soup into them, and tried to get water. And I sponged them. But my-- I had a very severe problem. You see, there was a hiding place, a very good hiding place in our house, that my brother Zachary helped build when we moved into this place. But I could not take my mother and my brother there. Because with this high fever, they were hallucinating and screaming.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I had to find some kind of place to-- to hide us in case there was an action. And the only place I knew was to just dig a hole under our-- one of our beds. So I spent nights digging holes-- this hole. And I-- I made myself pockets under my skirt and put the earth in. And then I used to go all over the ghetto and just let it loose.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because even if you were building a hiding place in the ghetto, you can't-- couldn't just put the soil in the backyard. Because when they came at an action and they saw fresh soil, they knew somebody had dug a hiding place, and they would demolish the house to find the people. So I had to spread it all over wherever I could. And I was digging furiously. Because we never knew when an action will start. It always started sometime in the middle of the night suddenly.

speaker: interviewer
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Who was taking care of your mother and your brother while you were gone?

speaker: interviewee
text: The-- the people in the house and-- and Bella-- Bella-- this wonderful Bella. If there was ever a hero, Bella was a hero. Bella came into the ghetto from a small village. Bella was a tall lady with beautiful red hair and green eyes. She had a sister, Rachel, who also had red hair and green eyes. She had two children, Daniel and Hannale. Daniel was about was 8-years-old at the time and Hannale was about 2, 3-- she was just a little infant. She-- she lived in the village. Her parents were-- her father and mother were killed. He was a furrier. And she took her sister and the two children, and she made a hole and made a hiding place in the forest. And she used to go around the villages and collect food. Because her father had given-- made furs for all of these-- these people. And they-- they gave her food. But then one time, the farmers didn't want to give her food anymore. And they followed her into the forest. And they caught her, a sister, and her two children, and put them on the wagon, tied them up--

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you know all this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Rachel told me later on the whole story-- and tied them up and they were going. And the farmer was going to bring them to the city of Buczacz, to the police, to have them killed. But the farmer wanted to monkey business with her on the way and took her off the-- off the wagon. And she was-- she carried a whip in her boots. And then she got herself disengaged from this farmer, took out a whip, and whipped that farmer really badly, and left him there-- actually, naked. And she took the horses and the wagon and drove into the ghetto. And I met them later on in the ghetto. And Rachel told me the whole story. And I thought Bella was a wonderful person. She was very kind to me. She was.

speaker: interviewer
text: So going back to your own life-- your mother and your brother still have typhoid. What happens?

speaker: interviewee
text: And then they got better. Just when they passed the crisis, one night there was a knock on the window that an action is starting, that the ghetto is surrounded, that they are going to gather us and take us to-- to the graves. And so what I did is I put a blanket into that hole, put my mother in. And then I lifted my little brother into her arms. And I decided that I am going to lay down on top of this opening. I will take the boards I removed for-- to dig-- and put them in front of me, hoping that if somebody poked under the bed, maybe they will think they were touching the wall. But even at the age of 12, I realized this is not going to work.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then I got a splendid idea. You know, the Germans always called us names like damn Jews, and [NON-ENGLISH], and the stinking Jews, and all kinds of ugly names, which didn't bother me. Because after all, who are they to call me names? But I thought this would be a very good opportunity to return a compliment-- to tell these murdering Germans what I thought of them.

speaker: interviewee
text: You see, he didn't have indoor plumbing. We had an outhouse. And we used a pail there.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I took the pail, and I spilled it all over the floor of our bedroom and in the other bedrooms. And I said to myself, OK. When you murderers come, I hope you slip and break your necks. So this was after you came back?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, that was after I came back. It-- it was very shortly after I came back that this action started.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you tell me how many people were living in the house?

speaker: interviewee
text: 16. All of--

speaker: interviewer
text: What were their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: The-- the Rosenberg family was there. And they had two daughters, two sons-in-law. And there was the baby, Schmulick, that died already. And then one was going to have a baby. And there were just some other people. I don't remember their names. But I-- I and then I went and laid down under the bed. And very soon, the whole house shook. And the door flew open to our room. And from under the bed, I could see those boots there. And then they were standing there and cursing, [NON-ENGLISH]. And one of them says, we'll get them next time. We'll get them next time. But they never entered our room. And then many hours later, maybe 48 hours later, when I-- I looked from the bed and I saw people walking on the streets, I knew the action was over.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so I knocked on the hiding place, on the regular hiding place. And the landlord came out. And he sniffled around, he and his daughter. He says, what is this terrible smell in this house, Alicia? So I told them what I did. And he patted me on the head and said, oh, you are a smart girl, Alicia. Now clean it up. And I cleaned it up.

speaker: interviewee
text: But you know, this house never smelled right again. It was a lost cause, because it all went into the wood there. It wasn't-- but I was very happy. I was able to save my mother and my little brother.

speaker: interviewer
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And what happened then?

speaker: interviewee
text: In very-- that was 1943. Very soon after, I returned. I was home a very short time. When the Germans declared that this city is going to be Judenrein-- that means it cleaned of Jews. The Jewish people cannot live in Buczacz any more. The ghetto is going to be canceled, and the people can go to one place I know of-- to Kopyczynce, to the ghetto of Kopyczynce. I imagine there must have been another place, what I wasn't aware of. So my mother and my little brother, and I went to the ghetto of Kopyczynce. And then we--

speaker: interviewer
text: Sorry. Before you go to Kopyczynce, is there any other action that sticks out in your mind that you'd like to tell me about?

speaker: interviewee
text: Any particular action? Well, we had-- we had several actions during that time. One, the first-- our first hiding place was in the wall. It was a stone wall that my brother built. And in order to close it, my brother had to stand there and actually hold the stone from the opening. And they were-- And they were hitting the wall. And they didn't hit that particular place where the opening was. And of course, the-- the thing I want to-- would like to share with you is my-- my very great disappointment, of course, that we children couldn't go to school. But what happened is it was still the beginning, in 1941, when the Christian children went back to school. I gathered my Jewish friends, and I decided that I'm going to make a statement. And we all walked into our classroom and sat in our seats. And when the teacher walked in and she saw us there, she got pale to her face. And she said, I'm sorry. You know, because you are not allowed to go to school. You all have to leave the classroom.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I-- I loved my teacher. I didn't want to give her any problems. So we left. But on the way back, I thought there must be some way I could continue learning. I really loved learning.

speaker: interviewee
text: We had a tree growing very close to the window of our classroom. So the following day, around 6 o'clock in the morning, I climbed into that tree, and I sat shivering in the cold leaves until the sun came out. And then at 7:30, the teacher opened the window, and there I was. I saw the blackboard. I saw my teacher. I saw my friends. And I was learning for quite a few weeks. Until one day, the teacher put an algebra question on the blackboard. She was looking for a common denominator. And I knew the number. And I forgot totally where I was, and I raised my hand. But I raised the wrong hand, and I came tumbling out of the tree. And of course, I screamed. And the teacher came. And the she looked at me and she says, Alicia, you cannot go in the classroom, and you cannot go into that tree.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I told her, all right. I say, I will not go into the tree again. But one day, one day if I live through the war, I'm going to go to school forever. And you know, I go like to school forever.

speaker: interviewee
text: And also about Milek who came to Buczacz.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is Milek?

speaker: interviewee
text: Milek is a great hero, a wonderful boy. I spent my summers in my uncle's home. And from the age of five, I wanted to be a physician like my uncle. That was my-- my dream. And this is what I was going to be. My uncle had a clinic attached to his home. He was a heart specialist, and this is where his patients were. And this boy, Milek, who was two years older than myself, decided at his age of seven that he is going to be a doctor.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he used to come and visit my uncle. And we became very good friends. And sometimes he used to catch a cat, and we bandaged up the cat. We were pretending they were our patients. So from the very young age, I fell in love with Milek, and I knew that someday we will both be free physicians and serve humanity.

speaker: interviewee
text: When his family was killed, he came to the city of Buczacz. And he helped my mother when I was away in the Chortkov ghetto, the Chortkov prison. He helped my mother. And then when we went to Kopyczynce--

speaker: interviewer
text: So-- so--

speaker: interviewee
text: --he also went to Kopyczynce.

speaker: interviewer
text: --so could you now continue telling me how you left--

speaker: interviewee
text: --OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: --to Kopyczynce?

speaker: interviewee
text: We left on the wagon to Kopyczynce. We were of the early people who left. Later on, I heard terrible things. And so people-- the people who left after we left were caught at-- at the entrance of the city and taken straight to the graves to be murdered.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you, yourselves?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was with my mother and my little brother. And Milek came with his friends. And we settled in into the Kopyczynce ghetto. And we were there just settling in a short while when the Germans declared that this ghetto is also going to be canceled out. The city of Kopyczynce is going to be Judenrein. But they didn't give us a place to go. They didn't say, well, from Kopyczynce you can go to another ghetto. This is where we are going to die.

speaker: interviewer
text: You know, you might rightfully ask me, Alicia, how come you didn't escape to forests, or to-- to villages, some place where you could save your lives, right? It's a good question.

speaker: interviewee
text: But you see, some of us did escape. But we were caught there by the Ukrainians and Poles, and we were literally chopped to pieces. This is the feedback we had from people who-- what happened to them. Some escaped already when they went into the forest. They had to come back to the ghetto, because they couldn't live in the forests themselves. The danger was there, right there from the Ukrainians and Poles.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did the Poles and the Ukrainians react to these ghettos?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Kopyczynce-- I didn't see them in Kopyczynce. I did not see them in Kopyczynce at all. We were just in the ghetto. I did not go out of the ghetto in Kopyczynce at all. This was a very strict ghetto, and I-- I wasn't long enough there. But the only thing I saw--

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you then?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was, at that time, not even 13. I was 12. I was still 12.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your mother like by now?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother was, of course, very slender. She started having gray in her hair. And her eyes, her beautiful violet eyes, were so sad. She still smiled for us, for my brother and for me. But she was very, very sad. I-- I could see times that she had cried. Her eyes were-- but she didn't do it in front of us.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did she tell you?

speaker: interviewee
text: She didn't talk about-- about it. But sometimes she used to tell us stories when she was a young girl in Sweden, and how she learned. She was a linguist. She knew all the languages. She was a language teacher. She tried to-- to sew somethings. She tried to trade for-- for food. She-- she tried to help other children, because people were coming in constantly from other places. They can enter that ghetto. She was trying to help other people.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did she tell you about the future?

speaker: interviewee
text: She-- whenever we talked, she said, Alicia, you must try to live-- to tell what happened. It was such a need in her. I always sensed that my mother believed that I will-- will be able to tell it. It's interesting, because now as an adult, she had such tremendous faith in me. But sometimes she also told me when she was a little girl, the things that happened to her. She tried to, but she wouldn't let me paint. You see, I was a very accomplished artist. She would never let me paint, because when I started painting it was a lot of red, a lot of blood. That's all I could see in-- in this world. So she was very careful.

speaker: interviewer
text: So going back to your life-- what happened then exactly?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Kopyczynce--

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: --very soon after we settled in, the Germans declared that this is also going to be Judenrein. And then one early morning, we were told that the ghetto is surrounded-- that this is going to be the end of us. And I remember I was in the hall, and the man in charge of the-- of the building there made a statement. He said, this is a-- there is a hiding place in this home, but it's very small. So if a mother and father had two children, only one child could go in. And you know, when I heard them say that, I caught his arm, and I told him, don't tell my mother. There were two boys who also had sisters. Don't tell our mothers. We will not go in. I give you my word of honor. I won't go in. And I actually kissed his hands. I said, please, don't tell my mother. We will not go in. And he looked at me with such sorrow. And he says, OK. I trust you, Alicia. And so I tried this. When people were going into the hiding place that was in the cellar, I tried to kind of stay away and hide myself. And my mother found me. She was so angry at me. This is the first time in my life that my mother was-- would have hit me. And she says to me, you should know better than procrastinate. You know that minutes make a difference between life and death. She didn't hear this man say about the-- the fact that two children couldn't go in. And so she dragged me down. We were the last of the people to come in. And then there was an opening-- a wooden-- a wooden frame. And people-- it was a tunnel under it. And people were catching each other.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I told my mother that she should go, and she will catch me. And I will take the wooden box and close the hiding place. I will try to put stuff on it and close it.

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother was holding up her arms. And I told her, if she lowered her arms, it will be easier for me to go in. The minute she lowered her arms, I took the wooden box and closed the opening. And I heard her scream that pierced me in my heart. And then I took all kinds of junk that was in that cellar there, and I put it on top of that area. Hoping that, you know, if I disguise it, nobody's going to think that people are hiding under this junk. And I went looking for the boys, hoping that maybe we could find a hiding place together or something. But I didn't find them. And I decided the only place I could go at this point-- maybe I will go to the attic. And maybe I will find a place. What I really wanted to do is to disappear into thin air. As I was climbing on the ladder, suddenly, I heard a baby cry. See, there were two babies in our house. And we found out through our experience that babies could not be taken into hiding places. Because they would cry, and would-- and-- and-- and the German's would hear the babies. And they would find the hiding places.

speaker: interviewee
text: So very often, the babies were given a strengthy drug and hidden. The mother would be knocked out by the father and taken into the hiding place. And the babies would have to-- to hope that they will survive. Now in this case, I had to make up my mind. Am I going to climb to the attic, or am I going to try to save the baby? And I decided to save the baby. So I returned, and I went into the room. There were two babies. I loved these babies. I made friends with them.

speaker: interviewee
text: And there was this little ball with the tea. It was-- the tea was in a little-- in a little sack. And I took the ball, and I was dropping it in the baby's mouth. One of the babies was sleeping. And-- and I was urging them. Hurry up, fall asleep so I can save you. And the baby was closing the eyes. And it would-- would have been just a matter of one minute. But suddenly, the whole house shook. The door flew open to our room. Two SS men entered, and with all this terrible noise, both babies woke up and started to cry. And one of the-- the Germans, one of the SS men, went ever-ward-- were-- went over where the babies were, pulled out his gun, and shot the babies through the mouth.

speaker: interviewer
text: In front of you?

speaker: interviewee
text: In front of me-- just like that. And I said something, and one of them hit me. And I don't know why they didn't kill me. But they-- they-- [NON-ENGLISH]. They gave a command out. And then they took me to the prison yard. And then people started coming, all being taken all over the ghetto. And then I found people from the Buczacz ghetto who--

speaker: interviewer
text: After that incident, what were you like?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was in shock. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I was angry. I hated them. There was such a burning sensation in my heart.

speaker: interviewee
text: I say to myself, there must be some way that they have to pay for their crimes. There was such anger and such hatred in my heart for them. How could they do something like that? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Could you describe the two German officers who did that?

speaker: interviewee
text: What they looked like? They had green uniforms on. They had the-- the swastika on. They had the SS emblem. They-- to me, they looked like devils. You know, they portray them in-- in-- in the movies and in television-- tall, blonde, with those blue eyes, these leather coats. To me they were ugly rats-- murdering cowards. That's what they were.

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- what were their faces like when did this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ugly-- pure ugly.

speaker: interviewer
text: And after they did that, what did they look like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ugly-- talked ugly.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did they say?

speaker: interviewee
text: They didn't say anything to me.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did they say to each other after they killed the babies?

speaker: interviewee
text: They didn't say anything. They didn't say. They pushed me out. [NON-ENGLISH]-- out. And then I joined the other people that were taken out-- just ugly murderers-- cowards. You--

speaker: interviewer
text: And so what did you do next?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was taken to a prison, and they put us in the prison yard. They gathered us there. And then the following morning, they took us out to the graves. But I want to tell you what happened. I sat near a mother who had two children-- a little-- a baby boy maybe-- maybe-- 18 months, or something like that. She was just holding him; and the little girl, 5-- she told me she was 5. I sat near her-- a beautiful a little girl.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the little girl turned to her mother, and she says, [NON-ENGLISH]. Mommy, where are we going? And the mother says, [NON-ENGLISH]. We are going to Daddy.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the little girl jumped up. And she was saying, we are going to Daddy. We are going to Daddy. The following morning, when they pushed us out, the mother was holding the baby. And she was walking in the front, and I was holding the little girl's hand. In the other hand, she was holding her doll. Somebody was shot in back of us, and we were pushed forward. The little girl dropped the doll. And one of the SS men-- we were walking on the-- one of the SS men came up, picked up the doll, handed it to the little girl. She smiled at him. And then he took out his gun and shot her face away. And I picked the little--

speaker: interviewer
text: In front of you.

speaker: interviewee
text: --in front of me. And I picked the little girl, and I carried her in my arms. I couldn't just leave her there. And I carried her to the grave.

speaker: interviewer
text: And her mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her mother never knew what happened.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was she?

speaker: interviewee
text: She was in front of us. Because you know, when you walk, a lot of people pushing you. And then I brought her to the grave with me. I want you to know something very important-- extremely important. Because we are--

speaker: interviewer
text: In the next tape. We're going to the next tape now.

speaker: interviewee
text: Ah, OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.
